data.title: WHY PLAN MODE MATTERS.
blocks: The cheapest defense against the dangerous middle is plan mode.

data.title: GIVE CLAUDE A WAY TO VERIFY ITS OWN WORK.
blocks: A pattern from Anthropic's own engineering guidance: when your specification includes a way for Claude to check its work
Give Claude a way to verify its own work — For the syllabus page, the checks:
data.section: GIVE CLAUDE A WAY TO VERIFY IT

S08.meta: // SLIDE 08 — TEACHER BUILD: THE FIRST DEPLO

data.title: TEACHER BUILD: THE FIRST DEPLOYABLE PAGE
blocks: · Compose the specification. Five elements. 100–200 words. For the first page of your site that does not yet exist.
· Run in plan mode. Shift+Tab twice. Submit the specification as your prompt. Read the plan.
Edit the plan. Ctrl+G. Make at least one change a path, a constraint, a step. Save. Return.
· Switch out of plan mode and execute. Watch the agentic loop run.
· Verify against the specification. Did Claude produce what the output-format element described? Did it respect the negati

data.title: CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS BUILD THEIR FIRST DEPLOYABLE ARTIFACT
blocks: · All five elements present and specific.
· The task is executed through Explore → Plan → Implement → Commit.
· The plan is edited via Ctrl+G before approval (mandatory; the activity teaches the editing move).
· Commit message captures the specification's intent.

data.title: COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS
blocks: "Specifications are for big builds."
"More words = better specifications."
"I can just edit the generated code."
"Plan mode slows me down."
"Negative constraints feel paranoid."
